title: Fainting at a crowded event

chart.oneLiner: 28F presents after a brief loss of consciousness while standing in a hot, crowded room, preceded by nausea and lightheadedness.

opening: I fainted at a concert — one minute I felt sick and dizzy, the next I was on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 28-year-old woman, now feeling back to normal, mildly embarrassed. Good historian.

HPI: Was standing for a long time in a hot, crowded room. Felt a clear PRODROME — warmth, nausea, lightheadedness, tunnel/graying vision, sweating — then briefly lost consciousness. Witnesses (if asked) say she was pale, out for <30 seconds, no jerking beyond a couple of brief twitches, came around quickly and was immediately oriented (NO prolonged confusion). Has had a couple of similar near-faints with prolonged standing or blood draws. NO chest pain, NO palpitations before, NO exertional trigger, NO tongue biting or incontinence.
PMH: none. MEDS: none. SOCIAL: student, no drugs, occasional alcohol. Last menstrual period normal (if asked).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, fully recovered.
- Vitals: BP 116/72, HR 74, RR 14, 36.9°C, SpO2 99% RA; mild orthostatic drop without significant symptoms.
- Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, NO murmur.
- Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Provide witness details (prodrome, brief duration, rapid recovery) only when the student asks about what witnesses saw or the prodrome/recovery.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Every sinus beat followed by a wide premature ventricular complex (ventricular bigeminy).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Every sinus beat followed by a wide premature ventricular complex (ventricular bigeminy). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Tortuous, unfolded thoracic aorta with calcification of the aortic knob.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Tortuous, unfolded thoracic aorta with calcification of the aortic knob. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing tortuous calcified aorta

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for the syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Vasovagal / neurocardiogenic syncope (leading,8). Orthostatic hypotension (core,3). Cardiac arrhythmia (CANT-MISS to exclude,4). Structural cardiac disease (CANT-MISS,3). Seizure (core,2). Hypoglycemia/anemia/pregnancy (core,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — clear prodrome, trigger, rapid recovery (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Vasovagal / neurocardiogenic syncope. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Cardiac arrhythmia; Structural cardiac disease. Also reasonable: Orthostatic hypotension; Seizure; Hypoglycemia/anemia/pregnancy.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — clear prodrome, trigger, rapid recovery

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG (CANT-MISS — exclude arrhythmia/conduction disease,6). Orthostatic vital signs (3). Fingerstick glucose (2). CBC for anemia (2). Pregnancy test in a woman of childbearing age (2). PENALTY: extensive cardiac/neuro imaging (CT head, echo, tilt-table, prolonged monitoring) in a classic low-risk vasovagal faint (-3 for over-testing); FAILING to get an EKG (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Glucose; CBC; BMP; Pregnancy; Orthostatics.

penalties.item: Extensive cardiac/neuro imaging (CT head, echo, tilt-table, prolonged monitoring) in a classic low-risk vasovagal faint

contraindicatedTests: Extensive cardiac/neuro imaging (CT head, echo, tilt-table, prolonged monitoring) in a classic low-risk vasovagal faint

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = ventricular bigeminy (each sinus beat coupled to a wide premature ventricular complex). CREDIT: rate & rhythm (5); identifies the premature ventricular complexes (4); recognizes the bigeminal pattern — every sinus beat followed by a PVC (4); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the bigeminy (-2).
commonMistakes: counting the PVCs into the underlying rate
mistaking bigeminy for an irregular atrial rhythm
steps.idealAnswer: Ventricular bigeminy: each sinus beat coupled to a wide PVC.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = tortuous, unfolded thoracic aorta with calcification of the aortic knob (chronic atherosclerotic change). CREDIT: identifies the tortuous/unfolded aorta with calcification (6); recognizes it as benign chronic atherosclerosis rather than acute pathology (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / mistaking the unfolded aorta for dissection (-2).
keyFindings: recognizes it as chronic atherosclerotic change
commonMistakes: mistaking an unfolded aorta for mediastinal widening from dissection
steps.idealAnswer: Atherosclerotic, tortuous thoracic aorta with a calcified aortic knob.

coreActions: recognizes it as chronic atherosclerotic change

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: With a normal EKG and a classic story, what is your diagnosis and how do you risk-stratify?
steps.rubric: Vasovagal syncope (6). Applies a risk-stratification framework — young, clear prodrome/trigger, normal exam and EKG = low risk (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Vasovagal (neurocardiogenic) syncope. Risk-stratify as low risk: young patient, clear prodrome and trigger (prolonged standing, heat, crowd), rapid spontaneous recovery, normal exam, and a non-ischemic EKG with no high-risk conduction features — safe for discharge without admission.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH syncope (reflex): reassurance/education – benign (CANT-MISS,6); trigger avoidance (5); physical counter-pressure maneuvers (4); ensure hydration/salt (3); confirm low-risk features + normal ECG before discharge (4). PENALTY: admitting/over-testing a clear low-risk vasovagal event (-2); missing a high-risk feature (exertional, no prodrome, abnormal ECG) (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Reassurance and education – benign prognosis. Counsel on avoiding triggers (prolonged standing, heat, dehydration), recognizing prodrome, and physical counter-pressure maneuvers (leg crossing, hand grip). Ensure hydration and salt intake. No cardiac admission if low-risk history and normal ECG. Reserve medications for recurrent refractory cases.

coreActions.item: Confirm low-risk features and normal ECG

optionalActions: Confirm low-risk features and normal ECG

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 28F presents after a brief loss of consciousness while standing in a hot, crowded room, preceded by nausea and lightheadedness. The diagnosis is Vasovagal syncope, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: regular, normal S1/S2, NO murmur.
Vitals: BP 116/72, HR 74, RR 14, 36.9°C, SpO2 99% RA; mild orthostatic drop without significant symptoms.
Neuro: fully oriented, no focal deficit, no tongue laceration.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Vasovagal syncope while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Vasovagal syncope and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
